title: Learning to Learn
objective: Students will explore learning to learn concepts including Understanding Why, Learning from Mistakes, Describing Rules & Patterns and more.

steps.content: Let's explore Understanding Why! Have you ever wondered about this?
Go beyond knowing *that* something is true — ask *why* it is true and *how* it works
Let me show you something cool. Ask 'why?' or 'how does that work?' about something they've been told, rather than just accepting it
Does you often ask 'but why?' when they learn a fact — wanting to understand the reason behind it rather than just accepting it?

Great job! Today we learned about Understanding Why. Ask 'why?' or 'how does that work?' about something they've been told, rather than just accepting it Explain the reason behind a rule or method — e.g. 'We carry the ten because 13 ones is the same as 1 ten and 3 ones'
Let's explore Learning from Mistakes! Have you ever wondered about this?
When you get something wrong, investigate why — what did you misunderstand or overlook? Analysing errors is one of the most powerful ways to learn
Time to try this out: error analysis research
When you gets something wrong on a test or activity, do they look at the mistake to understand what went wrong — rather than just moving on?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Learning from Mistakes: error analysis research Hattie feedback effect size
Today we're going to learn about Describing Rules & Patterns. Get ready for an adventure!
When you notice a pattern repeating, describe it as a rule that works every time — then test whether the rule holds in new cases
Let me show you something cool. Describe a pattern or rule they've noticed in their own words — e.g. 'Every time you add 0 to a number, it stays the same'
If you spots a pattern — like a grammar rule or a number pattern — can they describe it as a general rule and then check whether it works in new examples?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Describing Rules & Patterns: Describe a pattern or rule they've noticed in their own words — e.g. 'Every time you add 0 to a number, it stays the same' Test whether a rule they've described holds for new examples they haven't seen before
Let's explore Transferring Skills! Have you ever wondered about this?
Recognise when a skill or strategy learned in one subject or situation can be applied in a completely different one
Let's practice together! transfer of learning research
Has you ever spotted that something they learned in one subject — like a way of organising information — could help them in a completely different one?
Great job! Today we learned about Transferring Skills. transfer of learning research Perkins & Salomon transfer taxonomy
